text: # Kenya County Climate-Risk Notes Short, curated context the agent can retrieve alongside the satellite signals. Every statement here is paraphrased from the public sources listed in `sources.md` (NDMA bulletins, ACAPS, and open-access flood studies). Keep this file factual and attributed; it is part of the grounded-retrieval corpus. ## Drought phases (ASAL counties) Kenya's National Drought Management Authority classifies arid and semi-arid counties into phases: Normal, Alert, Alarm, Emergency, and Recovery, based on environmental, production, access, and utilization indicators.
source: county_notes.md

text: rm, Emergency, and Recovery, based on environmental, production, access, and utilization indicators. In early 2026, counties including Mandera, Wajir, Kilifi, and Kwale were classified in the Alarm phase, while Turkana, Marsabit, Samburu, Isiolo, Baringo, Laikipia, Tharaka Nithi, Kajiado, Taita Taveta, Kitui, Tana River, Garissa, and Lamu were in the Alert phase. (Source: NDMA National Drought Early Warning Bulletin, 2026.) ## Tana River flooding The Tana River regularly bursts its banks during the long rains (March–May), with flood risk heightened when the upstream Seven Forks dams are full.
source: county_notes.md

text: the long rains (March–May), with flood risk heightened when the upstream Seven Forks dams are full. Settlements in Garissa, Lamu, and Tana River counties are repeatedly exposed. Flooding in the lower basin damages crops, livestock, and infrastructure, and is associated with outbreaks of waterborne disease such as cholera. (Sources: ACAPS Kenya Floods briefing, 2024; NDMA.)
source: county_notes.md

text: outbreaks of waterborne disease such as cholera. (Sources: ACAPS Kenya Floods briefing, 2024; NDMA.) In Madogo Ward (Tana River County), studies record flooding roughly every two years, with topography (low-lying land near the river) as the dominant vulnerability factor; communities cope mainly by relocating to higher ground. (Source: Odoyo et al., 2024, CC-BY 4.0.) ## Population exposure and equity A large share of the population in the lower Tana basin lives in flood-prone households because livelihoods depend on the river for farming and water.
source: county_notes.md

text: basin lives in flood-prone households because livelihoods depend on the river for farming and water. Vulnerability is unevenly distributed — differentiated by age, gender, and disability — which is why ranking counties by physical hazard alone is insufficient and a transparent, exposure-aware risk score is used here. (Sources: Tana River flood-governance and vulnerability literature; see `sources.md`.) ## Coastal and western flooding Coastal counties (Mombasa, Kilifi, Kwale) experience flash flooding from intense short rains.
source: county_notes.md

text: ooding Coastal counties (Mombasa, Kilifi, Kwale) experience flash flooding from intense short rains. In western Kenya, Budalangi (Busia County) is among the most flood-prone areas due to the overflow of the Nzoia River. (Source: NDMA; regional flood records.)
source: county_notes.md

text: ORIGINAL ARTICLE Diversified flood governance and related socio-spatial vulnerability in Tana River County, Kenya Anita Nyapala Okoko, PhD Fellow Norwegian University of Life Sciences, Norway Correspondence Anita Nyapala Okoko, Norwegian University of Life Sciences, Elizabeth Stephansens v. 15, 1433 Ås, Norway. Email: anita.okoko@nmbu.no or nyapala.anita@gmail.com Abstract What are the consequential socio-spatial vulnerabilities of fragmentation in flood risk governance (FRG) functions in Tana River County, Kenya?
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: lnerabilities of fragmentation in flood risk governance (FRG) functions in Tana River County, Kenya? To answer the question, this paper utilises social differentiation theory to establish types and con- sequences of such fragmentation . It applies theoretical pat- terns and processes of social d ifferentiation to expose that FRG diversification could result in fragmentation of FRG func- tions. The study employs an interview strategy that focuses on governmental and non-governmental FRG actors.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: he study employs an interview strategy that focuses on governmental and non-governmental FRG actors. The results reveal that fragmente d FRG functions in Tana River County was not an intentional policy decision, but rather a product of a transitional vacuum between policy promulgation and implementation. Furthermore, socio-spatial vulnerabilities differentiated by patterns and processes related to age, gen- der, disability, and ethnicity, for instance, are consequential outcomes of fragmented FRG functions in the Tana River floodplains.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: for instance, are consequential outcomes of fragmented FRG functions in the Tana River floodplains. The study recommends implementation of the National Disaster Management Policy in Tana River County and institutionalisation of a specific FRG policy and legal framework.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

Disasters. 2024;48:e12648. wileyonlinelibrary.com/journal/disa 1o f1 9 https://doi.org/10.1111/disa.12648 1 | INTRODUCTION Floods are a frequent cause of massive devastation (Jonkman, 2005; Nygren, 2016). People choose or have no other choice but to live in dynamic coastal zones and floodplains in pursuit of the ecosystem services therein, which fuel their livelihood activities (Imeson, 2012).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: in pursuit of the ecosystem services therein, which fuel their livelihood activities (Imeson, 2012). People who live in coastal floodplains are at risk of the impacts of climate change and other economic developments that affect riverine and tidal flooding (Shampa et al., 2021). While the flood source is the river channel, to cause devastation, characteristics of overflow, such as speed of onset, magni- tude, spatial extent, and depth, must interact with human settlements exposed and vulnerable to such features (Franke, 2004; Wisner et al., 2014; Montz, Tobin, and Hagelman, III, 2017).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: nerable to such features (Franke, 2004; Wisner et al., 2014; Montz, Tobin, and Hagelman, III, 2017). Therefore, in the face of climate change, flood risk governance (FRG) is not only a matter that flood risk management (FRM) actors and decision-making process es must address to reduce or mitigate the impacts of flooding. In addition, it also poses a challenge to adaptati on and transformation, because it concerns overseeing FRM strategies for actions regardin g flood defence, prevention, mitigation, protection, and recovery (Gralepois, 2020).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: ctions regardin g flood defence, prevention, mitigation, protection, and recovery (Gralepois, 2020). FRG includes actor networks, discourses, an d multi-level coordination mechanisms in the pursuit of FRM (Alexander, Doorn, and Priest, 2018). The classical or traditional FRG pattern centres on a prevention-oriented approach, focu sing on infrastructural flood defences such as dams and dikes (De Bruijn and Klijn, 2001 ; van Popering-Verkerk and van Buuren, 2017 ). The classical or traditional FRG pattern concen- trates on infrastructure.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: k and van Buuren, 2017 ). The classical or traditional FRG pattern concen- trates on infrastructure. An alternative integrated approach has its foundations in integrated watershed man- agement, especially for arid and semi-arid areas, such a s the Tana River County in Kenya, where flexibility is required to manage water scarcity in periods of dr ought and surpluses in times of floods (Bouwer, 2002).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: red to manage water scarcity in periods of dr ought and surpluses in times of floods (Bouwer, 2002). Diver- s i f i c a t i o no fF R Gs t r a t e g i e si sw i d e l yr e g a r d e da san e c essary step forward to lessen the likelihood and magni- tude of flooding, as well as to minimise the exposure of people and property, and in turn, the disruption, economic damage, health impacts, and other adverse co nsequences that ensue when floods occur (Gilissen et al., 2016).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: ealth impacts, and other adverse co nsequences that ensue when floods occur (Gilissen et al., 2016). However, despite the political a nd instrumental efforts associate d with the diversification of FRG, the ramifications of floods are still devastating (Gralepois, 2020). Not all diversification of FRG affects socio-spatial vulnerability. However, a likely and inevitable outcome of diversification is that it can lead to fragmentation within FRG function areas and strategies, which can prove detri- mental to the effectiveness of FRG (Gilissen et al., 2016).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: s and strategies, which can prove detri- mental to the effectiveness of FRG (Gilissen et al., 2016). Hence, in many diversified strategies, FRG turns into a mat- ter of adaptation and self-responsibilitisation, which does not account for the socio-spatial distribution of vulnerabilities (Nygren, 2016). By overlooking the socio-spatial distribution of vulnerabilities, fragmentation in FRG strategies could create social inequalities. Existing scholarship on social inequalities links differentiation to social hierarchisation, and differ- ence to social inequalities (Lee and Juteau, 2003, pp.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: iation to social hierarchisation, and differ- ence to social inequalities (Lee and Juteau, 2003, pp. 8, 10). Hierarchisation in this case is the distribution of respon- sibilities between actors and governing rules enacted within different policy domains in FRG, which affect social inequalities. Lee and Juteau ( 2003) state that to understand the processes of social differentiation and its outcomes, social differences need to be comprehended and explained.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: f social differentiation and its outcomes, social differences need to be comprehended and explained. Apropos of flood protection governance within an institutionally fragmented environment, the development of public–private financing and implementation mechanisms for flood prevention and preparedness are differentiated by functions and areas, and the degree to which safety or risk of flooding is considered as optimal (Broekhans and Correljé, 2008).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: degree to which safety or risk of flooding is considered as optimal (Broekhans and Correljé, 2008). A novel finding is that social differentiation in flood impacts is relatively small soon after a flood, but widens over time, with socially disadvantaged groups enjoying less recovery (Houston et al., 2021).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: widens over time, with socially disadvantaged groups enjoying less recovery (Houston et al., 2021). Thus, in flood- plains such as the Tana River Delta, where decisions concerning financing and implementation of FRM mainly focus on the emergency (Okoko, 2022b), socio-spatial vulnerabilities owing to long-term flood impacts are easily over- looked in FRG and therefore continue to worsen with each flood experience.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: impacts are easily over- looked in FRG and therefore continue to worsen with each flood experience. In addition, changes in flooding resulting from climate change impacts may also alter the spatial differentiation of vulnerability depending on the sen- sitivities and adaptive capacities of groups inhabiting floodplains (Coulibaly et al., 2015).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: e sen- sitivities and adaptive capacities of groups inhabiting floodplains (Coulibaly et al., 2015). text: h as flash floods followed by long spells of drought (Kairu, 1997; Opere, 2013; Gitau et al., 2021). This paper uses Tana River County, Kenya, as a case to contribute knowledge on the consequences of diversified FRG and resultant fragmentation for socio-spatial vulnerabilities. It aims to explore how fragmentation in FRG for the Tana River catchment affects socio-spatial inequalities, which reflect in socio-spatial vulnerability to floods.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: atchment affects socio-spatial inequalities, which reflect in socio-spatial vulnerability to floods. To do so, this paper triangulates results from key informant interviews conducted within state and non-state agencies in the Tana Delta Sub-County with a document review of existing FRM policies in Kenya, including Tana River County. The paper then utilises social differentiation theory to establish the types and consequences of fragmentation in FRG in Tana River County.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: tiation theory to establish the types and consequences of fragmentation in FRG in Tana River County. Ultimately, it seeks to answer the following research question: ‘What are the consequen- tial socio-spatial vulnerabilities of fragmentation in FRG functions in Tana River County, Kenya? ’ 1.1 | Theoretical background Priority 2 of the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (SFDRR) 2015 –2030 articulates strengthening disas- ter risk governance to manage disaster risk.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: ion (SFDRR) 2015 –2030 articulates strengthening disas- ter risk governance to manage disaster risk. 1 The SFDRR emphasises the importance of mainstreaming and integrat- ing disaster risk reduction (DRR) within and across all sectors to review and promote coherence — coherence in disaster risk governance, however, was a pre-existing challenge in African countries (Sorell, 2013).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: disaster risk governance, however, was a pre-existing challenge in African countries (Sorell, 2013). While the SFDRR calls for integrated FRG, diversification in FRG strategies is a European Union policy and project concept focused on flood resilience, seeking to distinguish strategies of prevention, protection, preparedness, emergency response, and recovery (Oosterberg, Drimmelen, and Van der Vlist, 2005; Hegger et al., 2013). A major problem with the diversifi- cation of FRG strategies is that each way of doing so will not be feasible everywhere (Hegger et al., 2014).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: f FRG strategies is that each way of doing so will not be feasible everywhere (Hegger et al., 2014). Diversification of FRG may also imply that new or more actors acquire a role in FRM, adding complexities and poten- tial conflicts of interest (Broekhans and Correljé, 2008; Meijerink and Dicke, 2008; Hegger et al., 2014). Therefore, FRG actors have distinct roles and interests, as well as different policy issues of concern in terms of contributions to the resilience of the floodplain.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: as different policy issues of concern in terms of contributions to the resilience of the floodplain. Actors may differ in their interpretation of what socio-spatial vulnerabilities there are and how they should address them, which may hinder any changes at all that would benefit groups and individuals living in and depending on the floodplain.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: changes at all that would benefit groups and individuals living in and depending on the floodplain. Governance consists of the ways in which multiple and different actors behave, operate, interact, and confront each other in relation to the decision-making process on public affairs, which includes risks and disasters (Forino, Bonati, and Calandra, 2018). The concept of fragmentation in governance concerns the extent to which the world is composed of distinct clusters or divisions, regional or sectoral, of governmental organisation-based cooperation (Greenhill and Lupu, 2017).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: ns, regional or sectoral, of governmental organisation-based cooperation (Greenhill and Lupu, 2017). These distinct divisions attend to distinct functions, such as FRG functions. Therefore, they facilitate different decision-making processes and either shared or specialised responsibilities pertaining to FRM. These responsibilities concern specific FRM strategy actions and thus require specific competencies. Fragmen- tation in this case goes beyond institutions and their FRG functions to encompass their responsibilities and individual and/or holistic competencies for tackling FRM.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: ns to encompass their responsibilities and individual and/or holistic competencies for tackling FRM. Flood risk is a regional governance issue because it happens in geopolitical regions, countries such as Kenya, and in specific sections of such regions, including river catchments such as the Tana River. It is also a sectoral governance issue because flood impacts affect sectors such as food and agriculture, health, energy, housing, wildlife, trade and commerce, education, and transportation.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: d agriculture, health, energy, housing, wildlife, trade and commerce, education, and transportation. When FRG functions are integrated, there is central coordination region- ally and between sectors and strategies (Gilissen et al., 2016). When FRG functions are diversified, regional and sec- toral authorities, public, private, and non-profit, must cooperate, each bringing their own contributions and interests to FRM strategies (Hegger et al., 2014; Gilissen et al., 2016). Diversification in FRG functions thus translates into diversification in FRM strategies.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: al., 2016). Diversification in FRG functions thus translates into diversification in FRM strategies. In some cases, the focus will shift to what involved actors must do, and how they must do it. This shift depends on their institutional structures and processes, which alienates them from the recipients of FRM and the residents of floodplains, as well as their socio-spatial vulnerabilities (Nygren, 2016). text: which in turn link with the socio-spatial vulnerabilities of floodplain inhabitants. Gilissen et al. ( 2016) identify four main situations of fragmentation in FRG in Europe:  Type 1 fragmentation refers to situations in which distinct actors operating in different policy domains pursue dif- ferent FRM strategies. The entire existence of FRG is to ensure that the strategies in different policy areas for FRM work together effectively to reduce vulnerability and manage risk.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: in different policy areas for FRM work together effectively to reduce vulnerability and manage risk. However, when each policy domain has different actors and they act autonomously or semi-autonomously without a central coordinating agency, harmonisation of FRM outputs may be difficult to coordinate and achieve.  Type 2 fragmentation refers to situations in which distinct actors operating in the same policy domain pursue the same FRM strategy.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: ituations in which distinct actors operating in the same policy domain pursue the same FRM strategy. This scenario presents a situation where FRG actors address the same policy issue; with poor coordination or communication there could be poor implementation of strategies because each actor would think that the other should be responsible.  Type 3 fragmentation refers to situations in which distinct actors operating in different policy domains pursue the same FRM strategy.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: uations in which distinct actors operating in different policy domains pursue the same FRM strategy. This has similar consequences to the one above except that it will involve specific activities with regard to FRM strategies.  Type 4 fragmentation refers to situations in which distinct actors operating in the same policy domain pursue different FRM strategies. In this case, there could be poor harmonisation between strategies that are dependent on each other, or delays, owing to financing or resource allocation for one activity that could result in delays in other activities. Understanding the patterns, categorisations, and hierarchisation behind the political, economic, cultural, and normative processes of FRG fragmentation could be essential in explaining how socio-spatial inequalities and, in turn, vulnerabilities can be traced to applying diversified FRG in floodplains, where it may not be feasible. text: Patterns, categorisations, and hierarchisation are concepts in the theory of social differentiation. Social differentiation theory assumes that societies internally are highly diverse and differentiated, and differ- ences proceed from the more general to the more special (Albert, 2016, p. 48). The theory in its original form views functional differentiation, which equates to fragmentation, as a positive step in development.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: views functional differentiation, which equates to fragmentation, as a positive step in development. Fragmentation allows the more specialised functional units to make changes and decisions easily concerning their functions and strategies— functional differentiation has had proven success in solving former fundamental social problems (Albert, 2016).

The first subsection looks at the history of diversified disaster risk governance in Kenya and issues surrounding how diversi- fied FRG still exists in FRM even after the promulgation of the National Disaster Management Policy (NDMP) in 2009, which contained strategies for an integrated approach to disaster risk management. In addition, it also illus- trates the fragmented functions and roles.

The second subsection explores socio-spatial inequalities among groups and locations in the Tana River floodplains. The third subsection reveals current challenges due to fragmented FRG functions and FRM gaps in policy and practice, including a focus on drought management over flood management.

source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: ter issues and ensure that operations are legitimate, as well as to report to the SFDRR Secretariat. From the interview, it was clear that flood disaster issues are blanketed under other disasters, except for drought, and that their governance is responsive and fragmented among all actors involved in FRG, includ- ing national government line ministries, county government ministries and departments, private sector institutions, regional government authorities, international and local NGOs, CBOs, and faith-based organisations (FBOs).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: al government authorities, international and local NGOs, CBOs, and faith-based organisations (FBOs). A review of the NDMP reveals that the policy did not lead to diversified FRG. In fact, it calls for integrated man- agement of disasters and mainstreaming in development processes. From the time of the NDMP's promulgation in 2009 to the flagship of the NDMU, the NERP, and the SOPs in 2013 and 2014, there was a transitional vacuum in FRG in Kenya. First, the policy design was a centre –periphery system and could not be easily translated into a devolved system of government without an amendment.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: system and could not be easily translated into a devolved system of government without an amendment. In addition, the National Disaster Management Act was not in place— it remains a bill. Consequently, the system of fragmentation of FRG functions filled the transitional vacuum and became the norm, dominated by flood emergency rescue and response activities. In addition, there was not, and there still is not, a flood management policy or authority in Kenya.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: In addition, there was not, and there still is not, a flood management policy or authority in Kenya. NDOC correspondence indicated that in other floodplains, such as the River Nzoia floodplains, synonymous with the Budalangi floods of 2007, the WRA applied its integrated water resource management framework to manage floods with the community. The system was successful, with the WRA coordinating resettlement, flood defences, the emergency warning system, and floodplain land use for agricultural production and water supply.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: the emergency warning system, and floodplain land use for agricultural production and water supply. However, in Tana River, NDOC revealed that FRG was a function shared not just by national and county govern- ment departments, but also by other stakeholders. For instance, KenGen (the electricity generation company manag- ing the Seven Forks scheme dams in the upper catchment of the Tana River), TARDA, international and local NGOs, and CBOs and FBOs all had specific projects in Tana River County.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: RDA, international and local NGOs, and CBOs and FBOs all had specific projects in Tana River County. Drought management, however, is different from other disasters because Kenya Vision 2030 and its ASAL Pol- icy recognises drought and institutes a special system to govern it. The NDMA addresses drought risk with clear structures, funding, and response and monitoring mechanisms. In arid and semi-arid lands such as Tana River County, a focus on drought in policy encourages dryland farming close to settlements or in riverine/low-lying areas for local food security.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

However, it is clear from the develop- ment agenda of Kenya, fragmentation in food security strategies for droughts and other disasters, such as floods, leads to conflicting disaster risk management functions. Drought management policy encourages people to settle and farm on riverbanks for food security; an action that puts them at risk during floods.

text: nforce existing and reproduce new socio- spatial inequalities, as presented in the subsection below. 1.3.3 | Socio-spatial inequalities The results pointed to five patterns of socio-spatial inequalities, related to: age; gender; ethnicity; regionality; and disability. Age-related patterns of socio-spatial vulnerability were higher among children and the elderly. Rescue of the latter was a challenge because available rescue boats and personnel are deficient of the accommodations they require for boarding and transit.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: escue boats and personnel are deficient of the accommodations they require for boarding and transit. Among children, current FRM strategies do not safeguard rights and access to edu- cation services. For example, lorries ferry school feeding programme food, but they cannot cross floodwaters, leaving the children who can get to school to study on an empty stomach. In addition, there was no means of safe transpor- tation from marooned villages to school, putting children at risk of drowning and wildlife attacks.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: tation from marooned villages to school, putting children at risk of drowning and wildlife attacks. There was also no allocation to replace books, uniforms, backpacks, and other stationery swept away by floodwaters. Gender-related patterns were higher among women, especially among pregnant and lactating mothers. For instance, there were no enclosed bath areas in evacuee camps for women, who could not bathe in the open like men. Furthermore, there were no safe breastfeeding areas or special tents for expectant and nursing women.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

There was also no priority rescue tailored to pregnant and nursing women, because of the scarcity of rescue boats. This creates a spiral of overlooking inequalities, not learning and sharing information, repeating the same mistakes, having funding deficiencies, reproducing existing differences (vul- nerabilities and inequalities), and producing new forms of differences, trapping floodplain residents in a never-ending vulnerability web. 2 | DISCUSSION Not all diversified FRG results in detrimental fragmentation, though, especially where it is intentional, and policy-led, as in cases in Europe.

text: al fragmentation, though, especially where it is intentional, and policy-led, as in cases in Europe. In such instances, a policy provides the template for amendment and clear, coordinated institu- tions within which rectification of competency and responsibilitisation issues is possible. This case study, however, presents one of the least studied scenarios of unintended or unplanned fragmentation in FRG functions, especially in Sub-Saharan Africa. Leck et al.

In this study, unintended fragmented governance is the nexus of inadequate monitoring of hazards, impacts, and vulnerability.

The processes of FRG decision-making and cooperation in the results reveal that while the role of diversified government policies and agencies is to manage floods, they could produce fragmented responsibility without clear coordination, which features entrenched bureaucracy and competition.

Consequently, the challenges of fragmentation and the resultant FRM gaps keep reproducing and producing socially differentiated socio-spatial inequalities and vulnerabilities among floodplain residents in Tana River County. These inequalities align with Lee and Juteau's (2003) patterns of social differentiation because they are either related to age, gender, ethnicity, culture, and regionality.

Addressing age- and gender-related patterns of vulnerability among women in FRG is important because in Afri- can countries most women lack access to land, livestock, and financial capital, which are critical in reducing vulnera- bilities to disasters (Chineka et al., 2019). A study in Dhaka, Bangladesh, pointed out that women, infants, and young children are the groups most vulnerable to flood impacts, both during the flood emergency and in its aftermath (Goudet, 2010; Goudet et al., 2023).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: d impacts, both during the flood emergency and in its aftermath (Goudet, 2010; Goudet et al., 2023). In Tana River County, the differentiation is not just between but also within groups. Among women there are women-headed households, expectant women, lactating women, and women living with disabilities and/or HIV/AIDS. Among children, the differentiation is between infants and school-going children, each vulnerable for varied reasons. In addition, recent studies show that elderly people are among the groups most vulnerable to the negative impacts of disasters (Bayraktar et al., 2018).
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

Another study concluded that the psychologi- cal and physiological health effects of floods appear to be borne disproportionately by females, the elderly, and chil- dren during floods (Lowe, Ebi, and Forsberg, 2013). Therefore, generalisation of relief aid, as well as a lack of post- flood strategies, increases the impact of floods on these groups and their vulnerability, which at times could mean loss of life.

This pattern of differentiation intri- cately links to loss of property, interruption of livelihoods, poor access to roads and markets, and poverty.

Thus, at the community level, there is resis- tance to FRM strategies such as resettlement plans.

In addition, the fragmentation challenge of poor information sharing has led to distrust of warnings among floodplain residents; most people choose to shelter in place until they can see the floodwater rising. This is a clear illustration of the disconnect between experts and laymen, as pointed out by Clausen ( 1992).

This would ensure that integration or diversification of FRG functions is intentional, and policy-led, and therefore it is easy to configure exact points of fragmentation in FRG functions, institutional challenges, and implementation gaps and address them effectively. Implementation of the NDMP should include the development and enactment of the National Disaster Management Act, which is not yet in place, and a clear implementation framework for disaster management in Kenya.

The framework should comprise specific disaster management policies for each disaster, simi- lar to the ASAL Policy, the inclusion of these policies in the country's development agenda, Vision 2030, to ensure financing, the creation of relevant institutions, and staffing and equipping of these institutions with competencies and capacity to monitor and reduce risk, and prepare for, respond to, and recover from specific disasters in their realm.

These institutions should establish a way to coordinate with county and regional authorities and other actors on FRG at local levels, including floodplain residents, through information sharing, the deployment of staff and equip- ment, and planning, monitoring, and evaluation, to prevent the FRM gaps that materialised in this study.

To tackle the socio-spatial differences established here, the study recommends that FRM policies include a clause that ensures representation of these groups in institutions and in FRG decision-making processes, especially for emergency response. The exacerbation of the socio-spatial vulnerabilities in this study, especially those related to age and gender, could just be a beginning.

text: ulnerabilities in this study, especially those related to age and gender, could just be a beginning. Involving these groups will enable them to present and address all of their needs pertaining to flood response and recovery and avoid overlooking them, as in the current fragmented FRG sys- tem and functions. In addition, since most socio-spatial differences in vulnerability increase because the FRG system overlooks interventions in the aftermath of floods, there is need for a clear flood recovery strategy, with relevant financing, rebuilding, oversight, and public participation.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: r flood recovery strategy, with relevant financing, rebuilding, oversight, and public participation. This study also recommends the establishment of permanent rescue centres in the floodplains, equipped with necessities for all groups, as well as wheelchair access and child-friendly boats and rescue vehicles. In the event of a flood, floodplain residents, especially those in marooned and hard-to-reach areas, can then shelter in place without worrying about provisions. It also recommends protection dikes in the floodplains as well as raised permanent hous- ing, where practical and possible.
source: Disasters - 2024 - Okoko - Diversified flood governance and related socio‐spatial vulnerability in Tana River County  Kenya.pdf

text: ection dikes in the floodplains as well as raised permanent hous- ing, where practical and possible. Governance actors could also empower and enable local communities to plant trees along riverbanks, as well as flood crops as buffers and for food security. At governance levels, there is a need for oversight of private actors, such as KenGen in Kenya, which manages and uses the river catchment and claims to prevent floods to ensure that its actions are as flood control-based as they are profit-based.

The same oversight should apply to donor agencies and NGOs to ensure that their actions do not aggravate socio-spatial vulnerabilities. Also of benefit would be a clear emergency warning system that is timely and accurate regardless of which FRG actor relays information to the public, to foster trust in FRG institutions and the adoption of FRM strategies by flood- plain populations.

text: to foster trust in FRG institutions and the adoption of FRM strategies by flood- plain populations. 3 | CONCLUSION This study set out to answer the following research question: ‘What are the consequential socio-spatial vulnerabil- ities of fragmentation in FRG functions in Tana River County, Kenya? ’. The literature from Europe revealed that extensive scholarship exists on fragmentation of FRG functions as part of intended diversification of FRG and limited studies exist on unintended diversification of FRG, which this paper addresses.

text: n of FRG and limited studies exist on unintended diversification of FRG, which this paper addresses. The scenario of unintended fragmen- tation of FRG functions in the study area, Tana River County, exists because of the delayed implementation of the existing NDMP. This policy set out to tackle the challenges of fragmented FRG functions, such as funding, monitor- ing, accountability, competing interests, data and information sharing, politics, preparedness, avoidable losses of life, and donor issues. This study revealed a link between these challenges and FRM gaps.

Despite these short - term improvements, hot and dry conditions persisted in several areas, sustaining drought risk and slowing full recovery of livelihoods.

Observed Drought Indicators 2.1.1 February 2026 Rainfall Performance Rainfall performance across the ASAL counties in February 2026 showed a slight improvement compared to January, though the spatial and temporal distribution remained uneven and largely inadequate to significantly ease prevailing drought stress. Most areas received light to mo derate rainfall, providing only short-term moisture gains and limited recovery of rangeland resources.

text: Cattle prices remained generally stable across most counties during the reporting period (Table 4). Health and nutrition The nutrition situation, as assessed through the Mid -Upper Arm Circumference (MUAC) measurements among children under five years, indicated a generally stable trend across the majority of the ASAL counties during the month under review. However, deteriorat ion in the nutrition status was reported Garissa, Isiolo, Wajir, Embu, Kilifi, Narok, and Tharaka Nithi (Table 11).
source: National_Drought_Early_Warning_Bulletin_Feb_202620260311171955.pdf

text: ition status was reported Garissa, Isiolo, Wajir, Embu, Kilifi, Narok, and Tharaka Nithi (Table 11). The observed stability in most counties was partly attributed to improved access to nutrition services through integrated health outreach pr ogrammes, which continued to support routine screening and early identification of malnutrition cases. Conversely, the worsening trend reported 14 in the affected counties was associated with limited access to diverse diets, as well as declining household milk consumption, which reduced the availability of key nutrients for young children.
source: National_Drought_Early_Warning_Bulletin_Feb_202620260311171955.pdf

Overall, the proportion of children classified as ‘at risk’ o f malnutrition was above the usual seasonal range in approximately 52 percent of the counties. The elevated proportions were mainly linked to persistent poor health -seeking behaviour, high morbidity levels, and suboptimal child care and feeding practices, which continue to influence child nutrition outcomes in several ASAL areas.
